**ADC SOLUTIONS**

**Facility-Side CDU to External Heat-Rejection System**

**Bill of Materials / Bill of Design Package**

Louisiana hot-humid climate basis with cold-climate comparisons

Scope: Project Deschutes CDU primary/facility-side interface \-\> facility PCW loop \-\> external heat rejection system

| Field | Value |
| :---- | :---- |
| Document No. | ADC-FAC-HRS-BOM-001 |
| Version | 0.1 \- Draft RFQ / BoD issue |
| Date | May 19, 2026 |
| Prepared for | ADC Solutions / Louisiana high-density water-cooled compute deployment |
| Basis CDU | OCP Project Deschutes 2 MW CDU; 500 GPM facility side; 500 GPM IT side; 3 C HX approach target |
| Excluded by request | Absorption chillers. Electric chillers are included only as a secondary / trim / backup option. |
| Classification | CONFIDENTIAL \- ADC Solutions Internal & Authorized Vendors Only |

| Procurement decision summary For Louisiana, procure a clean closed primary water loop from the CDU to an external evaporative heat-rejection system. The recommended first architecture is a closed-loop evaporative fluid cooler or a campus open-tower plus plant plate-HX arrangement, depending on scale. Chillers should be optional trim/backup modules, not the first heat-rejection method. Do not route open tower water directly through the CDU primary side. |
| :---- |

# **1\. Document Intent and Source Basis**

This package creates a facility-side Bill of Materials and Bill of Design for the path from the Project Deschutes CDU primary/facility connections to the external heat-rejection system (HRS). It is written as an RFQ-ready guideline for Louisiana deployments and includes cold-climate contrasts so the design team can avoid applying cold-climate assumptions to a Gulf Coast site.

| Source ID | Source / guideline used | Design impact |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| D1 | OCP Project Deschutes v1.0, effective Feb 26, 2026 | Locks the CDU performance basis: 2 MW, 500 GPM primary/facility flow, 500 GPM secondary/IT flow, 15-30 psi facility-side dP, 3 C HX approach, DI water or PG25 compatibility, and facility water pipe-kit validation. |
| D2 | ADC Modular TCS BoD/RFQ package | Confirms the TCS secondary/rack-side scope is separate from primary/facility PCW and that quantities should scale per 2 MW CDU / reference row. |
| D3 | Deschutes Redfish / AI Control Reference | Defines Tier 3 facility and Tier 4 external monitoring roles, and keeps the CDU PLC as deterministic safety/control authority. |
| D4 | OCP Open Data Center Spec v0.5.0 | Frames why high-density ML deployments need day-one facility capacity and why liquid cooling is the thermal interface for modern ML rows. |
| W1 | Louisiana climate / weather references | Uses Louisiana hot-humid conditions as the primary basis; final values must be confirmed using the project weather station and current ASHRAE climatic design data. |

| Boundary rule This BOM does not alter the Deschutes CDU internal pump selection, heat exchanger configuration, control valve architecture, secondary TCS layout, or rack-side interface. All pumps and heat-rejection equipment in this package are external facility-side equipment. |
| :---- |

# **2\. Executive Recommendation \- Louisiana First**

Louisiana is a hot-humid climate for design purposes. That matters because dry coolers are limited by high dry-bulb temperature, while evaporative equipment is limited by high wet-bulb temperature. For warm-water, liquid-cooled compute, evaporative heat rejection can still be very effective because the CDU does not require traditional 44 F chilled water. The facility plant should target the highest CDU primary supply temperature that keeps the IT secondary supply within rack/cold-plate limits and maintains the Deschutes HX approach requirement.

| Rank | Method | Louisiana use case | Why this ranking |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| 1 | Closed-loop evaporative fluid cooler on a clean PCW loop | Preferred for a first Louisiana Deschutes deployment or modular 2-10 MW block. | Keeps the CDU primary loop closed and clean while achieving practical warm-water supply temperatures during hot-humid conditions. Reduces contamination risk versus open tower water in the CDU loop. |
| 2 | Open cooling tower \+ plant plate heat exchanger \+ closed CDU PCW loop | Preferred for campus-scale plants when water treatment staff and tower O\&M maturity are available. | Open towers can reach low wet-bulb approach, but the tower water should be isolated from CDU PCW by a plant HX. Adds water treatment and Legionella program burden. |
| 3 | Hybrid dry / adiabatic cooler | Good water-saving or shoulder-season option; not recommended as the sole peak Louisiana system unless the CDU primary supply setpoint can be high. | Adiabatic assist helps, but Louisiana dry-bulb and humidity limit peak rejection. Often needs chiller trim or oversizing. |
| 4 | Electric chiller trim / backup | Secondary option only; include as modular trim or full backup if the business requirement demands it. | Useful when required CDU primary supply is below what the HRS can deliver or during HRS outage. High-temp chilled water / low-lift operation is preferred. |
| Not included | Absorption chillers | Excluded by user direction. | Procurement complexity, lead time, serviceability, and site-utility dependencies make them unsuitable for this BOM. |

* Use water-side heat rejection first. Do not design the plant as a conventional low-temperature comfort chilled-water system unless the IT equipment actually requires it.  
* For one Deschutes CDU, size the HRS around 2.0 MW IT load plus heat-rejection margin, typically 2.1-2.2 MW planning capacity per CDU until final vendor data is available.  
* Keep the CDU primary/facility loop closed, filtered, treated, instrumented, and isolated from open tower water.  
* Use electric chillers as a secondary or trim stage, staged by wet-bulb forecast, CDU primary supply temperature, and IT load forecast.

![][image1]

***Figure 1\. Recommended Louisiana starting architecture: clean closed PCW loop with external evaporative heat rejection and supervisory facility telemetry.***

# **3\. Reference Design Basis \- One 2 MW Deschutes CDU**

The following values are used for BOM quantities. They are not a replacement for final hydraulic modeling, vendor heat-rejection selection, water treatment design, or stamped engineering drawings.

| Parameter | Value for BOM | Notes |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Thermal load per CDU | 2,000 kW IT load; 2,100-2,200 kW HRS planning capacity | The 5-10% HRS margin covers CDU/pump heat, fouling, sensor error, and selection margin. Final margin should be owner-defined. |
| Equivalent refrigeration tons | 569 RT at 2,000 kW; 597-626 RT at 2.1-2.2 MW | Used only for chiller/tower comparison; the primary plant should be warm-water optimized. |
| Facility-side flow | 500 GPM per CDU | Deschutes basis: 0.25 GPM/kW. |
| Facility-side CDU dP | 15-30 psi through CDU primary side | External pump head must include CDU dP plus field piping, strainers, valves, HRS coil/HX, and elevation/static requirements. |
| CDU HX approach target | 3 C / 5.4 F | Primary supply temperature should be approximately 3 C colder than required secondary supply at design load. |
| Facility water temperature rise at 500 GPM | \~27.3 F / 15.2 C at 2 MW with water | Example: 86 F PCW supply to CDU returns at roughly 113 F before HRS margin. |
| CDU operating liquid temperature range | 18-55 C / 64.4-131 F | Do not confuse this with final IT inlet limits; rack/cold-plate OEM limits govern. |
| Design pressure | 150 psig minimum for field-side CDU-adjacent hardware | Match or exceed CDU/facility pipe kit requirements and AHJ requirements. |
| CDU-adjacent pipe size | 6 in nominal supply and return per 2 MW CDU as starting basis | At 500 GPM, 6 in pipe keeps velocity near 5-6 ft/s depending on pipe ID. |
| Fluid basis | Closed PCW: treated water preferred in Louisiana; PG25 allowed where freeze/exposed-loop risk requires it | Glycol reduces heat transfer and increases pumping penalty; avoid unless needed. |

| Temperature planning example If the IT secondary supply requirement is 95 F and the CDU HX approach target is 5.4 F, the facility PCW supply entering the CDU should be about 89-90 F. That is feasible with evaporative heat rejection during many Louisiana design cases, but not with dry coolers on the hottest days unless the IT supply temperature allowance is higher. |
| :---- |

# **4\. Louisiana Climate Basis and Design Implications**

Louisiana design should begin with hot-humid assumptions. The State of Louisiana assigns the state to climate zone 2A for energy purposes, with East and West Carroll parishes assigned warm-humid treatment. Building America defines hot-humid regions using both precipitation and sustained warm wet-bulb exposure. NOAA/NWS 1991-2020 normals for New Orleans International Airport show July/August normal highs around 91 F, lows in the mid-70s F, and annual precipitation above 60 inches. Public ASHRAE design extracts for New Orleans show peak evaporation wet-bulb values near 81 F. Final equipment selection shall use the current ASHRAE Weather Data Viewer or owner-approved project weather file for the exact site.

| Climate factor | Louisiana design consequence | BOM consequence |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| High wet bulb / high humidity | Evaporative equipment still works, but leaving-water temperature cannot be assumed low. Design to warm PCW. | Use fluid cooler/tower selections based on 0.4% evaporation wet-bulb and high approach reliability. |
| High dry bulb | Dry coolers alone deliver high water temperatures in summer. | Do not make standalone dry coolers the peak design method unless IT allows very high coolant temperature or HRS is oversized. |
| High rainfall / Gulf Coast corrosion | Outdoor equipment sees moisture, salt/humidity, biological growth, and wind-driven rain. | Specify coated casings, stainless fasteners, NEMA 4X/3R enclosures, sealed controls, sloped pads, and corrosion-resistant supports. |
| Hurricanes / severe storms | Outdoor HRS can lose airflow, controls, water supply, or power. | Use anchoring, debris protection, emergency operating sequence, generator-backed controls/pumps as required by uptime target, and manual bypass/isolation. |
| Occasional freezing conditions | Not a cold-climate site, but exposed coils/piping can freeze during rare events or outage. | Include heat trace, drain valves, basin heaters, freeze stats, minimum flow, or glycol only where exposed-loop risk requires it. |
| Water availability and treatment | Evaporative methods consume water and require chemical management. | Include make-up water, blowdown, conductivity control, side-stream filtration, sample stations, and Legionella water management plan. |
| **Planning weather value** | **Preliminary value** | **How to use it** |
| Louisiana energy climate zone | 2A for energy purposes; selected parishes warm-humid | Use hot-humid assumptions for HRS and condensation risk. |
| New Orleans MSY 1991-2020 normals | July/August normal highs \~91 F; annual precipitation \~63 in | Confirms high sensible load and water-management environment. |
| New Orleans International public design extract | 0.4% evaporation WB/MCDB approx. 80.7 F / 88.7 F | Use as preliminary tower/fluid cooler sizing check; verify with current ASHRAE tool. |
| New Orleans Lakefront public design extract | 0.4% evaporation WB/MCDB approx. 81.4 F / 89.5 F | Lake/coastal sites may be slightly more wet-bulb constrained. Verify by site. |

![][image2]

***Figure 2\. Louisiana method ranking. Options are not additive unless the plant is intentionally designed as a hybrid system.***

# **5\. Procurement Structure and BOM Rules**

The BOM is organized as modular packages. Select one primary heat-rejection package (Package C, D, or E), plus common packages A, B, G, H, I, J, and K. Package F is optional electric chiller trim/backup and should be procured only when required by the thermal envelope or uptime model.

| Package | Required? | Package description | Counting basis |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| A | Yes | CDU facility water pipe kit and immediate tie-in hardware | Per Deschutes CDU |
| B | Yes | Facility PCW pump skid / pump set | Per CDU or shared per plant hydraulic zone |
| C | Recommended primary | Closed-loop evaporative fluid cooler HRS | Per CDU or per modular 2-10 MW block |
| D | Alternative primary | Open cooling tower \+ plant plate heat exchanger | Usually shared campus/block plant |
| E | Alternative / hybrid | Hybrid dry/adiabatic cooler | Per CDU or shared plant zone |
| F | Optional secondary | Electric chiller trim/backup module | Sized to trim fraction or full backup target |
| G | Yes | Facility piping, supports, civil, drainage, insulation | Per route and per site layout |
| H | Yes | Water treatment, filtration, make-up, blowdown | Per loop; open and closed loops separated |
| I | Yes | Instrumentation, controls, BMS/SCADA/Redfish bridge points | Per CDU and per HRS plant |
| J | Yes | Electrical power, VFDs, MCC, disconnects, grounding | Per equipment package |
| K | Yes | Commissioning, validation, O\&M spares | Per system |

# **6\. Detailed BOM \- Package A: CDU Facility Water Pipe Kit and Tie-In**

| Tag | Qty per 2 MW CDU | Component | Performance / selection specification | Procurement notes |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| FWK-01 | 1 | Primary supply isolation valve | 6 in full-port butterfly or ball valve; 150 psig minimum; low leakage; lockable handle or actuator-ready. | Place at CDU primary supply connection. Body material 316SS or coated ductile iron; avoid nickel-plated brass. |
| FWK-02 | 1 | Primary return isolation valve | Same as FWK-01. | Allows CDU isolation without draining the plant loop. |
| FWK-03 | 2 | Grooved or tri-clamp transition spool | 6 in nominal; 150 psig minimum; compatible with CDU connection details; include gaskets. | Use stainless near CDU. Match Deschutes optimized coupling approach. |
| FWK-04 | 2 | Flexible stress-relief spool or coupling set | Rated 150 psig minimum, 500 GPM, compatible with PCW chemistry and temperature range. | Use only where needed for alignment, vibration, or service pull-out. |
| FWK-05 | 1 | Primary inlet strainer | 6 in basket or Y-strainer; stainless basket; DP taps; blowdown valve; mesh final per CDU OEM. | Protects CDU primary valve/HX from construction debris. Provide bypass or service valves for cleaning. |
| FWK-06 | 1 | Primary loop flow meter | 0-600 GPM normal range; \+/-0.5% reading target; magmeter or ultrasonic; BACnet/Modbus output. | Align with Deschutes flow-meter accuracy expectations. |
| FWK-07 | 2 | Temperature sensors | Supply and return RTD or thermistor wells; accuracy suitable for approach calculation. | Install in wells for service. Provide BMS and CDU/AI readout. |
| FWK-08 | 2 | Pressure transducers | 0-150 psig or 0-200 psig range; isolation valves; local gauge taps. | Use to calculate CDU-side dP and diagnose fouling/flow restrictions. |
| FWK-09 | 1 | Manual air vent / high-point vent set | Stainless or compatible body; piped to safe location if needed. | Use at local high points. Avoid automatic vents over energized IT equipment unless piped. |
| FWK-10 | 2 | Low-point drains and sample ports | 3/4 in to 1 in valve; cap/plug; hose thread where appropriate. | Required for flushing, chemistry sampling, and winterization. |
| FWK-11 | 1 | Local leak-detection cable and drip tray allowance | Leak rope or point sensors under CDU tie-in; route to CDU/BMS alarm zone. | Facility side can leak near data hall; integrate with existing leak response plan. |
| FWK-12 | A/R | Insulation and vapor barrier | Closed-cell elastomer indoors if PCW supply can be below room dew point; aluminum/stainless jacketing outdoors. | Warm-water operation may not need indoor condensation insulation, but chiller trim can create condensation risk. |

# **7\. Detailed BOM \- Package B: Facility PCW Pump Skid**

These are external facility pumps. They do not replace or resize the Deschutes CDU pumps. The final pump curve shall be selected after pipe routing, HRS coil/HX data, control valve authority, strainer loss, and redundancy topology are known.

| Tag | Qty | Component | Performance / selection specification | Procurement notes |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| PMP-01 | 2 x 100% per CDU or 3 x 50% shared | PCW circulation pumps | 500 GPM per CDU at calculated TDH; include CDU primary dP 15-30 psi plus field losses; VFD duty. | Horizontal split case, inline, or end-suction based on plant layout. Use N+1 at the plant level. |
| PMP-02 | 1 per pump | VFD | Matched to motor; BACnet/IP or Modbus TCP; HOA; safe restart; harmonic mitigation as required. | NEMA 12 indoors, NEMA 3R/4X outdoors. Coordinate with emergency power. |
| PMP-03 | 1 per pump | Suction isolation valve | Full-size valve with lockable handle. | Allows service without draining the plant loop. |
| PMP-04 | 1 per pump | Discharge isolation valve | Full-size valve; balancing not by throttling unless designed. | Use VFD speed and control valves for normal control. |
| PMP-05 | 1 per pump | Check valve | Low-pressure-drop spring or silent check valve sized for 500 GPM. | Prevents reverse flow through standby pump. |
| PMP-06 | 1 set | Pump suction diffuser / strainer | As required by pump type; include serviceable screen and blowdown. | Do not duplicate restrictive strainers unnecessarily; account for dP. |
| PMP-07 | 1 | Minimum-flow / bypass assembly | Bypass valve or control logic to maintain pump/HRS minimum flow. | Needed for low-load commissioning, chiller trim, or cell isolation. |
| PMP-08 | 1 set | Pump skid base and vibration isolation | Structural steel skid, housekeeping pad, flexible connectors where justified. | Anchor for wind/flood/seismic per site. Provide drip containment if indoors. |
| PMP-09 | 1 set | DP sensor across plant supply/return | Range per hydraulic model; BMS point. | Used for variable flow reset and fault detection. |
| PMP-10 | 1 set | Local pump control panel | VFD control, safety interlocks, alarms, Ethernet, manual override. | BMS can supervise; local safeties must remain hardwired/deterministic. |

# **8\. Detailed BOM \- Package C: Closed-Loop Evaporative Fluid Cooler (Recommended Primary)**

This option uses a closed PCW coil connected to the CDU primary loop. Spray water evaporates outside the coil and does not enter the CDU primary loop. This is the preferred first Louisiana method for a modular deployment because it combines clean CDU water with evaporative heat-rejection capability.

| Tag | Qty | Component | Performance / selection specification | Procurement notes |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| HRS-C-01 | 1 N+1 module set | Closed-loop evaporative fluid cooler | 2.1-2.2 MW heat rejection per Deschutes CDU at project 0.4% evaporation WB; 500 GPM PCW; PCW supply target 85-90 F planning range. | Vendor to propose 3 x 50% cells, 2 x 100% units, or shared cell bank. Must prove full-load operation with one fan/cell/pump failure if uptime target requires it. |
| HRS-C-02 | A/R | Closed-loop coil | Stainless coil preferred for long life; copper or galvanized only with approved chemistry and materials review. | Keep CDU PCW closed and filtered. Confirm galvanic compatibility with stainless CDU pipe kit. |
| HRS-C-03 | N+1 | Fan array with VFD/EC control | Variable speed fans; corrosion-resistant motors; low-noise option if site requires. | Stage fans before chiller trim. Include fan power telemetry. |
| HRS-C-04 | N+1 | Spray water pumps | Sized per fluid cooler vendor; redundancy per uptime target. | Provide strainers and service valves. |
| HRS-C-05 | 1 set | Basin, fill, drift eliminators | Low-drift eliminators; basin access; cleanout; overflow and drain. | Include Legionella water management program and drift control. |
| HRS-C-06 | 1 set | Make-up water assembly | Backflow preventer, pressure regulator, solenoid/float control, meter, low-level alarm. | Coordinate with site water source, softening, chemical treatment, and storm mode. |
| HRS-C-07 | 1 set | Blowdown and conductivity control | Conductivity controller, actuated blowdown valve, flow meter or runtime tracking. | Target cycles of concentration per water treatment vendor; log data to BMS. |
| HRS-C-08 | 1 set | Chemical feed system | Biocide, scale/corrosion inhibitor, pH control as required by water treatment plan. | Use secondary containment and service access; include chemical tank level alarms. |
| HRS-C-09 | 1 set | Freeze protection | Basin heater, heat trace, drain-down, minimum flow, or glycol strategy as required. | Louisiana still needs rare-event/outage protection for exposed water. |
| HRS-C-10 | 1 set | Access platforms and service clearances | OSHA-compliant platforms, ladders, tie-off points, lighting if needed. | Critical for safe maintenance during humid/slippery conditions. |
| HRS-C-11 | 1 set | Hurricane / corrosion package | Coastal coating, stainless fasteners, wind-rated anchorage, debris/hail screens as needed. | Final requirements per structural engineer, flood elevation, and insurer. |

# **9\. Detailed BOM \- Package D: Open Cooling Tower \+ Plant Plate HX (Campus-Scale Alternative)**

This option is well suited to larger campuses with mature water-treatment operations. The open tower loop should be isolated from the CDU PCW loop by a plant plate heat exchanger; do not send open condenser/tower water directly to the CDU primary side.

| Tag | Qty | Component | Performance / selection specification | Procurement notes |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| HRS-D-01 | 1 N+1 cell bank | Open cooling tower | 2.1-2.2 MW per CDU equivalent or shared multi-CDU capacity; selected at current ASHRAE 0.4% evaporation WB. | Use multiple cells with isolation so one cell can be serviced while load continues. |
| HRS-D-02 | 1 per hydraulic zone | Plant plate heat exchanger | 2.1-2.2 MW per CDU equivalent; 500 GPM PCW side per CDU; tower water side per selection; 3-5 F HX approach planning. | Stainless plates; gasket material compatible with tower chemistry and PCW. Provide lifting and cleaning access. |
| HRS-D-03 | N+1 | Tower water pumps | Sized for tower loop and plate HX pressure drop; VFD control. | Separate from CDU PCW pumps. |
| HRS-D-04 | N+1 | PCW pumps | Closed-loop PCW pumps serving the CDU primary side. | May be shared with Package B. Avoid pump fighting by defining control authority. |
| HRS-D-05 | 1 set | Tower basin filtration / separator | Side-stream sand separator or media filter; basin sweeper if needed. | Important in Louisiana biological/dust/pollen conditions. |
| HRS-D-06 | 1 set | Tower water treatment | Conductivity, blowdown, make-up, biocide, antiscalant, pH, corrosion control. | Mandatory Legionella water management. |
| HRS-D-07 | 1 set | Drift / plume / intake separation review | Drift eliminators, stack extension if needed, and minimum separation from air intakes. | Coordinate with building intake locations and neighbors. |
| HRS-D-08 | 1 set | Winter / low-load controls | Bypass, basin heater/drain, minimum flow, fan cycling limits. | Even Gulf Coast towers need low-temperature protection. |

# **10\. Detailed BOM \- Package E: Hybrid Dry / Adiabatic Cooler (Alternative / Supplemental)**

Hybrid adiabatic coolers reduce water use but must be checked carefully in Louisiana. They are useful when water conservation is a hard constraint, as a shoulder-season stage, or as a supplement to evaporative or chiller plant capacity. They should not be assumed capable of peak 85-90 F PCW supply in hot-humid design weather without vendor proof.

| Tag | Qty | Component | Performance / selection specification | Procurement notes |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| HRS-E-01 | 1 N+1 module set | Hybrid dry/adiabatic cooler | 2.1-2.2 MW per CDU equivalent; 500 GPM PCW; vendor shall state dry-only and adiabatic-assisted capacity at site design weather. | Require clear capacity tables at 95-100 F DB and high RH, not generic catalog data. |
| HRS-E-02 | N+1 | Dry coil fan array | EC/VFD fans; corrosion-resistant motors; fan section isolation. | Fan power can be high in Louisiana; include energy model. |
| HRS-E-03 | 1 set | Adiabatic media or spray system | Water distribution, media, flush cycle, freeze protection, drain-down. | Media maintenance and water quality are critical in humid climates. |
| HRS-E-04 | 1 set | Water quality controls | Filtration, water treatment, conductivity and flush controls. | Adiabatic systems still need biological and scaling controls. |
| HRS-E-05 | 1 set | Bypass / chiller-trim interface | Controls to stage chiller trim when adiabatic cooler cannot meet PCW supply target. | Avoid waiting for CDU high-temperature alarms before staging trim. |

# **11\. Detailed BOM \- Package F: Electric Chiller Secondary Option**

Electric chillers are included only as a secondary option. Do not specify absorption chillers in this package. Chillers should be high-temperature, low-lift, variable-speed equipment where possible because the CDU primary loop is warmer than conventional comfort chilled water.

| Tag | Qty | Component | Performance / selection specification | Procurement notes |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| CH-01 | Option A: 25-50% trim; Option B: 100% backup | Electric chiller module(s) | Trim basis: 150-300 RT per 2 MW CDU equivalent. Full-backup basis: \~600 RT per 2 MW CDU equivalent, plus redundancy as required. | Air-cooled or water-cooled electric. High CHW leaving temperature capability preferred. No absorption chillers. |
| CH-02 | 1 set | Chiller interface plate HX or direct PCW evaporator connection | Use if chemistry or pressure requires separation; otherwise direct high-temp CHW may be allowed by engineer. | Avoid contaminating CDU PCW. Maintain service isolation. |
| CH-03 | 1 set | Chilled-water pumps | Variable flow; sized for chiller flow and pressure drop. | May be separate from PCW pump skid. Define control logic to prevent unstable flow. |
| CH-04 | 1 | Buffer tank / thermal flywheel | Sized to prevent short cycling and support fast IT load ramps. | Strongly recommended for modular chillers and ML load transients. |
| CH-05 | 1 set | 3-way mixing / injection valve assembly | Controls amount of chilled water injected into PCW or trim HX. | Allows HRS to carry base load while chiller trims only the deficit. |
| CH-06 | 1 set | Chiller controls integration | BACnet/IP or Modbus TCP; enable/disable, capacity %, alarms, leaving-water temp, kW. | Integrate with wet-bulb forecast and CDU primary supply trend. |
| CH-07 | 1 set | Power feeders and emergency-power review | Electrical distribution sized for chiller module(s). | Chiller backup has no value if not aligned with generator/UPS availability target. |

| Chiller staging sequence Stage chiller trim only when the evaporative/hybrid HRS cannot maintain the PCW supply temperature needed for the CDU approach target. For most warm-water liquid-cooled compute, this is a peak wet-bulb, maintenance, or low-temperature workload event; it should not be the default cooling mode. |
| :---- |

# **12\. Detailed BOM \- Common Packages G, H, I, J, K**

| Tag | Qty | Component | Performance / selection specification | Procurement notes |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| PIPE-01 | A/R | 6 in CDU PCW supply/return piping | 6 in nominal per 500 GPM CDU branch; 150 psig minimum; velocity target \~5-6 ft/s for 6 in pipe. | Use stainless near CDU/data hall. Outdoor yard piping may be carbon steel, HDPE, or approved material with chemistry review. |
| PIPE-02 | A/R | Aggregate PCW headers | Size by number of CDUs and velocity/dP targets; hydraulic model required. | Do not simply manifold many 500 GPM CDUs onto undersized mains. |
| PIPE-03 | A/R | Pipe supports and anchors | ASME B31.9/B31.1 as applicable; wind/seismic/flood review. | Include expansion loops or joints for long outdoor runs. |
| PIPE-04 | A/R | Insulation / jacketing | Outdoor UV/weather jacket; indoor vapor barrier when PCW below dew point. | Chiller trim can create condensation even if normal HRS mode is warm. |
| PIPE-05 | A/R | Drains, vents, fill/purge stations | High-point vents, low-point drains, hose connections, sample ports. | Needed for flushing, freeze response, and commissioning. |
| WQ-01 | 1 per closed loop | Closed-loop chemical treatment | Corrosion inhibitor compatible with CDU primary wetted materials; pH/conductivity limits by vendor. | Closed loop should be clean and stable; avoid untreated raw water. |
| WQ-02 | 1 per loop | Side-stream filtration | 5-10 micron nominal for closed PCW; larger basin/tower filtration as needed. | Use DP gauges and service bypass. |
| WQ-03 | 1 per open/spray loop | Legionella water management program | Documented program aligned to CDC/ASHRAE guidance; includes biocide, monitoring, cleaning, and records. | Required for cooling towers, evaporative fluid coolers, and adiabatic wetted systems. |
| WQ-04 | 1 set | Make-up water meter and blowdown meter | Pulse/analog outputs to BMS. | Needed for water balance, leak detection, and operating cost. |
| CTRL-01 | 1 per plant | HRS controller | Controls fans, pumps, spray, blowdown, basin heat, chiller trim signal. | Must fail safe and retain local manual control. |
| CTRL-02 | 1 per CDU/HRS zone | BMS / SCADA integration | BACnet/IP, Modbus TCP, or equivalent; trend all critical points. | Map to Tier 3 facility and Tier 4 external context. |
| CTRL-03 | 1 per site | Weather station | Outdoor dry bulb, RH, calculated wet bulb, wind, rainfall; optional lightning/storm inputs. | Install away from tower plume and reflective surfaces. |
| CTRL-04 | 1 set | Alarm logic and load-shedding interface | PCW supply high, PCW flow low, pump fail, fan fail, basin low, water treatment alarm, chiller unavailable. | Use warning bands before CDU temperature alarm. |
| ELEC-01 | A/R | MCC / VFD power distribution | Sized for pumps, fans, spray pumps, chiller options, heat trace, controls. | Consider generator-backed circuits for critical HRS loads. |
| ELEC-02 | A/R | Disconnects and local service receptacles | Lockable, labeled, NEMA-rated for environment. | Outdoor enclosures in Louisiana should be corrosion resistant. |
| ELEC-03 | 1 set | Grounding, bonding, surge / lightning protection | Per NEC, AHJ, insurer. | Important for large outdoor equipment and Gulf storm exposure. |
| CX-01 | 1 lot | Factory submittals and hydraulic model | Vendor curves, HRS capacity table, pump curves, controls points, water-treatment plan. | No release for fabrication until model proves 500 GPM per CDU and required PCW temp. |
| CX-02 | 1 lot | Flushing and cleaning | Flush until cleanliness criteria met; verify strainers clean; chemical startup. | Protect CDU HX from construction debris. |
| CX-03 | 1 lot | Hydrostatic / leak test | 150 psig minimum or per engineer/AHJ; document results. | Coordinate with CDU pipe kit and plant limits. |
| CX-04 | 1 lot | Functional performance test | Verify flow, dP, PCW supply temp, fan/pump staging, chiller trim, failover, alarms, BMS points. | Test at low, medium, and high load where possible. |
| SP-01 | 1 lot | Recommended spares | Pump seals/bearings where applicable, VFD spare, fan motor/drive, sensors, chemical probes, strainer baskets, gaskets. | Stock based on availability and site criticality. |

# **13\. Design Calculations and Sizing Methods**

| Calculation | Formula / method | Result for one 2 MW CDU | Design use |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Heat load conversion | 2,000 kW x 3,412 \= 6.824 MMBtu/hr; 1 RT \= 12,000 Btu/hr | 2 MW \= \~569 RT | Use for chiller/tower comparisons. |
| Facility water Delta-T | Delta-T F \= Btu/hr / (500 x GPM) | 6.824 MMBtu/hr / (500 x 500\) \= \~27.3 F | If PCW supply is 86 F, PCW return is \~113 F. |
| Heat rejection selection margin | Q\_HRS \= 2,000 kW x 1.05 to 1.10 | 2.1-2.2 MW planning capacity | Accounts for fouling and selection margin; owner to set final margin. |
| Pipe velocity check | V ft/s \= 0.4085 x GPM / ID^2 | 6 in Sch 40 ID \~6.065 in gives \~5.6 ft/s at 500 GPM | Supports 6 in CDU branch basis. |
| Evaporation estimate | Evap gpm approx Btu/hr / (1000 Btu/lb x 8.34 lb/gal x 60\) | \~13.6 gpm evaporation at 2 MW | Peak water-use planning for evaporative HRS. |
| Blowdown estimate | Blowdown \= evaporation / (cycles of concentration \- 1\) | At 4 cycles: \~4.5 gpm blowdown | Total make-up often \~18-20 gpm per 2 MW plus drift/leaks. |
| Tower/fluid cooler leaving water | Approx LWT \= design evap WB \+ equipment approach | 80-81 F WB \+ 6-9 F approach \= \~86-90 F | Explains why warm-water compute is a good Louisiana match. |
| Dry cooler leaving water | Approx LWT \= design DB \+ dry approach | 95-100 F DB \+ 10-15 F approach \= \~105-115 F | Explains why dry-only is weak for Louisiana peak. |

| Important: design temperature is a system decision The HRS cannot be selected until the allowed rack/cold-plate inlet temperature, desired secondary supply temperature, and CDU approach margin are known. A higher allowed IT coolant temperature reduces or eliminates chiller hours. A lower IT coolant temperature moves the plant toward electric chiller operation. |
| :---- |

# **14\. Operating Modes and Best Methods**

| Mode | Trigger | Equipment sequence | Best-method notes |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Normal Louisiana economizer mode | PCW supply target achievable by evaporative HRS | PCW pumps maintain flow/DP; fluid cooler/tower fans and spray modulate; chiller off. | Default preferred mode. Reset PCW supply upward when IT limits allow. |
| Peak wet-bulb mode | HRS near full fan/spray and PCW supply starts rising | Pre-stage all available HRS cells; open CDU primary valve under PLC limits; notify BMS/AI; stage chiller trim if configured. | Use forecast to act before CDU alarm. |
| Chiller trim mode | Required PCW supply below HRS capability | Run HRS as pre-cooler; inject chilled water or use trim HX to lower PCW supply only as needed. | Avoid full mechanical cooling when a few degrees of trim is enough. |
| HRS maintenance mode | Cell, pump, or tower service | Isolate one cell/module; remaining N+1 capacity carries load or chiller covers deficit. | Isolation valves and drain points must be accessible. |
| Storm / hurricane mode | Severe weather watch, high wind, utility risk, water interruption | Pre-cool within safe limits, verify water tanks/chemicals, lock down equipment, stage redundant pumps/fans, establish load-shed plan. | Do not rely on outdoor air-moving equipment during unsafe wind conditions unless rated and energized. |
| Low-load / commissioning mode | Partial IT load or startup | Use minimum-flow bypass, pump turndown, fan minimum speed, buffer tank/chiller anti-short-cycle logic. | ML loads can ramp quickly; do not over-optimize for steady low load. |
| Rare freeze event mode | Outdoor temperature near freezing or equipment idle | Maintain flow, heat trace, basin heat, drain-down exposed sections, or use glycol-protected exposed loop. | Louisiana risk is outage \+ cold snap, not long winter operation. |
| **Best method** | **Reason** | **BOM implementation** |  |
| Keep PCW closed from CDU to HRS coil/HX | Prevents biological solids and tower debris from entering CDU primary side. | Closed-loop fluid cooler or plant HX; strainers, side-stream filtration, chemistry control. |  |
| Design for warm water first | High-density liquid-cooled compute can often use warmer water than legacy chilled water. | Evaporative HRS primary, chiller secondary. Include PCW reset logic. |  |
| Use N+1 at plant elements, not just CDU pumps | A redundant CDU is not useful if one HRS fan cell or PCW pump failure trips the row. | N+1 pumps, cells/fans, isolation valves, bypasses, chiller trim if required. |  |
| Trend wet bulb and PCW approach | Wet-bulb forecast predicts HRS capacity before alarms occur. | Weather station, BMS trending, AI supervisory reset. |  |
| Protect against condensation when chiller trim is enabled | Warm-water mode may not condense; chiller trim can. | Vapor-barrier insulation indoors, dewpoint alarms, minimum supply temperature limits. |  |
| Water management is a reliability item | Evaporative capacity depends on clean heat-transfer surfaces and controlled biological growth. | Conductivity, biocide, anti-scalant, basin cleaning, Legionella WMP, filter maintenance. |  |

![][image3]

***Figure 3\. Facility HRS controls should coordinate with, but not bypass, the CDU PLC safety authority.***

# **15\. Cold-Climate Comparison**

The same Deschutes CDU facility interface can be served by a very different HRS in cold climates. In colder regions, dry coolers and water-side economizers may carry most or all annual hours, but freeze protection and overcooling control become dominant. This table is included to show why Louisiana should not be designed as a cold-climate dry-cooler plant.

| Design topic | Louisiana hot-humid approach | Cold-climate approach | What changes in the BOM |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Primary heat rejection | Evaporative fluid cooler or open tower \+ HX; dry-only not peak basis unless warm setpoint is high. | Dry cooler or waterside economizer often becomes primary; evaporative may be seasonal/off. | Louisiana BOM emphasizes water treatment and biological control; cold BOM emphasizes glycol, heat trace, drain-down, and mixing. |
| Design limiting weather | High evaporation wet bulb and high dry bulb. | Low ambient temperature, freezing wind, snow/ice, and overcooling. | Different sensors and safeties: LA wet-bulb forecast; cold freeze stats and coil protection. |
| Chiller role | Secondary trim/backup for peak wet-bulb or low-temperature IT requirement. | Often minimal if dry cooler can maintain PCW; may be used for summer only. | Cold sites may reduce chiller size; Louisiana may retain trim module. |
| Water use | Evaporative water make-up can be significant. | Dry cooler water use can be near zero in winter. | Louisiana needs make-up/blowdown meters; cold sites need glycol management. |
| Condensation risk | Usually controlled by warm PCW, but chiller trim can drop below data-hall dewpoint. | High if outdoor economizer overcools PCW. | Both need minimum supply temperature and dewpoint-aware controls. |
| Freeze protection | Rare-event protection. | Core design requirement. | Cold BOM adds glycol tank, heat tracing, drainable coils, basin heaters, emergency power for circulation. |
| Operational risk | Scaling, biofilm, corrosion, hurricanes, water supply. | Freeze rupture, overcooling, snow blockage, low-load stability. | Different spares and procedures. |

![][image4]

***Figure 4\. Planning-level operating envelope comparison. Vendor selections must be based on project weather files and final IT coolant limits.***

# **16\. RFQ / Vendor Submittal Requirements**

| Submittal | Required content | Pass / fail expectation |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Thermal capacity table | Capacity at site 0.4%, 1%, and 2% design conditions; dry-only, spray/evap, and chiller-trim modes as applicable. | Must show 2.1-2.2 MW per CDU equivalent at 500 GPM or state required flow/temperature limits. |
| Hydraulic model | Pump curves, system curves, valve authority, strainer/HX/coil dP, turndown. | Must prove 500 GPM per CDU and stable low-load operation. |
| Materials and chemistry statement | All wetted materials; CDU PCW compatibility; tower/spray water materials; gasket compatibility. | No unapproved nickel-plated brass in CDU-adjacent circuit; no material conflicts with selected chemistry. |
| Controls point list | All sensors, alarms, commands, trend intervals, BACnet/Modbus object list. | Must support Tier 3 facility and Tier 4 external monitoring. |
| Water treatment plan | Closed-loop inhibitor, open/spray loop biocide, blowdown, cycles, Legionella WMP, sample schedule. | Required for any evaporative or adiabatic equipment. |
| Resilience package | Wind rating, anchorage, flood elevation, corrosion coating, emergency power dependencies. | Must match Louisiana site hazards and insurance/AHJ requirements. |
| Commissioning plan | Flush, hydrostatic test, controls checkout, thermal performance test, failover test, water treatment startup. | Must include documented acceptance criteria. |
| O\&M plan and spares | Maintenance intervals, cleaning steps, filter/chemical/sensor spares, lead times. | Must be serviceable by local Gulf Coast vendors. |

# **17\. Open Engineering Items**

| Open Item | Owner / engineer decision needed | Why it matters |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| HRS-01 | Confirm the exact Louisiana site, elevation, weather station, design return period, and owner design margin. | HRS capacity and wet-bulb approach depend on site weather and reliability criteria. |
| HRS-02 | Confirm IT rack/cold-plate allowable inlet temperature and required secondary supply setpoint. | Determines whether evaporative HRS alone can carry peak load. |
| HRS-03 | Choose primary heat rejection package: C closed fluid cooler, D open tower \+ HX, or E hybrid dry/adiabatic. | Avoid double-counting equipment and conflicting controls. |
| HRS-04 | Define chiller role: none, trim only, or full backup. | Affects electrical service, generator requirements, controls, piping, and cost. |
| HRS-05 | Confirm redundancy target: N, N+1, 2N, or protected-state SLO. | Drives number of cells, pumps, chiller modules, and bypasses. |
| HRS-06 | Complete water quality analysis for make-up water and closed-loop fill. | Determines treatment chemicals, blowdown, filtration, and materials. |
| HRS-07 | Complete flood, wind, corrosion, and site civil review. | Critical for Louisiana outdoor HRS survival and serviceability. |
| HRS-08 | Finalize controls protocol and point naming: BACnet/IP, Modbus TCP, MQTT, Redfish bridge. | Needed for BMS/AI telemetry and commissioning. |
| HRS-09 | Determine whether PCW piping crosses data halls or sensitive electrical areas. | Determines leak detection, drip containment, insulation, and routing constraints. |
| HRS-10 | Define AHJ and insurer requirements for cooling towers, Legionella, chemical storage, and emergency power. | May change BOM items for containment, monitoring, and documentation. |

# **18\. Commissioning and Acceptance Criteria**

| Test | Minimum acceptance target | Documentation |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Pipe kit / field pipe pressure test | 150 psig or engineer/AHJ requirement without leakage; staged to protect CDU if isolated. | Pressure log, photos, signed witness sheet. |
| Flush and cleanliness | Strainers clean after flush; turbidity/particulate criteria per water treatment engineer and CDU vendor. | Flush report, filter/strainer photos, water analysis. |
| Flow validation | 500 GPM per CDU at design mode; flow turndown stable at low load. | Flow-meter trend, pump speed, dP readings. |
| Thermal validation | At available load, HRS meets PCW supply target; extrapolate with vendor curves if full load unavailable. | Temperature trends, fan/pump/chiller state, weather data. |
| Failover validation | Loss of one pump/cell/fan does not create uncontrolled CDU temperature rise in protected state. | Failover test script and trends. |
| Chiller trim validation if installed | Chiller stages on/off without short cycling; trim valve stable; no condensation alarm. | Chiller trend, buffer tank temp, valve position. |
| Controls point-to-point | All BMS/SCADA points read, alarm, and command correctly; local safeties override remote. | Point-to-point checklist. |
| Water treatment startup | Chemical residuals and conductivity in range; WMP issued; sampling schedule established. | Water treatment report and WMP. |
| Storm/freeze readiness | Heat trace, basin heaters, drain valves, tie-downs, and emergency sequences verified. | Seasonal readiness checklist. |

# **19\. References**

| ID | Reference |
| :---- | :---- |
| D1 | Open Compute Project, Project Deschutes: Data Center Facilities, v1.0, effective Feb 26, 2026\. |
| D2 | ADC Solutions, Modular Technology Cooling System (TCS) CDU-to-Rack Header & Branch Line BoD/RFQ Package, Version 1, May 19, 2026\. |
| D3 | ADC Solutions / Scott Tomsu, OCP Project Deschutes Redfish, AI Control & Four-Tier Data Center Management, May 2026\. |
| D4 | Open Compute Project, Datacenter: Next-Generation ML Infrastructure Design Principles, v0.5.0, effective Feb 25, 2026\. |
| W1 | Louisiana Administrative Code / International Energy Conservation Code amendment: all Louisiana climate zone 2A for energy purposes, with specified parish exceptions. |
| W2 | U.S. DOE Building America Best Practices, Guide to Determining Climate Regions by County, hot-humid definition. |
| W3 | NOAA/NWS New Orleans/Baton Rouge 1991-2020 Climate Normals. |
| W4 | ASHRAE Weather Data Center and public ASHRAE design-condition extracts for New Orleans stations; final design to use current ASHRAE Weather Data Viewer. |
| W5 | CDC, Controlling Legionella in Cooling Towers, updated Jan 2025, referencing ASHRAE Guideline 12 control measures. |

| Not-for-construction note This document is a procurement and design-basis package. Final equipment sizing, piping, structural anchorage, electrical feeders, water treatment, chemical storage, and code compliance require site-specific engineering and AHJ review. |
| :---- |
